findings.title: JSONDecodeError from the fast-path (UTF-detected branch) is not caught and re-raised as RequestsJSONDecodeError
findings.detail: When `guess_json_utf` succeeds and `complexjson.loads()` is called on line 899-901, a `JSONDecodeError` thrown there is swallowed only if it is a `UnicodeDecodeError` (line 902). A plain `json.JSONDecodeError` / `simplejson.JSONDecodeError` from that call is NOT caught — it propagates raw to the caller instead of being wrapped in `RequestsJSONDecodeError`. Only the fallback `complexjson.loads(self.text, **kwargs)` on line 910 is wrapped. This is a regression from the stated goal of making the exception type consistent.

findings.title: Python 2 branch uses `e.message` which is deprecated/unavailable for newer ValueError subclasses
findings.detail: `e.message` was deprecated in Python 2.6 and is not a guaranteed attribute on all `ValueError` subclasses. In Python 2, the canonical way to get the message string is `str(e)` or `e.args[0]`. If `e.message` is empty or missing, the re-raised `RequestsJSONDecodeError` will carry no useful diagnostic information.

findings.title: MRO / __init__ conflict when CompatJSONDecodeError is ValueError (Python 2)
findings.detail: On Python 2, `CompatJSONDecodeError` is `ValueError`. `JSONDecodeError(InvalidJSONError, ValueError)` inherits both `RequestException.__init__` (which pops `request` / `response` kwargs) and `ValueError.__init__`. `RequestException.__init__` calls `super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)`, which will eventually chain to `ValueError.__init__`. This should work as long as no extra kwargs are passed, but the interplay is fragile and untested. More critically, on Python 3 when simplejson is NOT installed, `CompatJSONDecodeError` is `json.JSONDecodeError`, whose `__init__` signature is `(msg, doc, pos)` — three positional arguments — while `RequestException.__init__` accepts `*args`. If Python's MRO calls `json.JSONDecodeError.__init__` with the wrong argument count (e.g., when constructed with a single exception object), a `TypeError` will be raised instead of the intended `JSONDecodeError`. The raise at line 917 passes `(e.msg, e.doc, e.pos)`, so that specific call is fine, but the class is fragile for any other instantiation path.

findings.title: New test only covers the fallback decode path, not the UTF-detected fast path
findings.detail: The test `test_json_decode_compatibility` uses `httpbin('bytes/20')`, which returns random bytes without a JSON Content-Type. This exercises only the `complexjson.loads(self.text)` fallback path. There is no test for the case where `guess_json_utf` succeeds and finds an encoding but `json.loads` still fails (e.g., valid UTF-8 bytes that are not valid JSON). Because the fast path is not wrapped (see the critical finding), that gap is also untested.

findings.title: Test does not verify inheritance / backwards-compatibility catches
findings.detail: The PR's stated goal includes backwards compatibility — catching `requests.JSONDecodeError` should also be catchable as `ValueError`, `json.JSONDecodeError`, and `requests.RequestException`. The new test only checks that `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` is raised; it does not assert that `except ValueError`, `except json.JSONDecodeError`, or `except requests.exceptions.RequestException` also catch it.

findings.title: simplejson's JSONDecodeError is used as the compat alias even though models.py no longer uses simplejson for decoding
findings.detail: The PR removes simplejson usage from `models.py` (switching to `complexjson` which is still `simplejson` when installed), but the alias in `compat.py` still imports `simplejson.JSONDecodeError` when simplejson is present. This is intentional for catch-compatibility, but it creates a subtle asymmetry: when simplejson is installed, the library uses simplejson for decoding (via `complexjson`) so the caught type is still correct. However, the comment in models.py (line 913) says "This aliases json.JSONDecodeError and simplejson.JSONDecodeError" which is misleading — it only aliases one of them at a time depending on whether simplejson is installed, not both simultaneously.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError imported from compat is unused in models.py after refactor (duplicate/redundant import)
findings.detail: `JSONDecodeError` is imported from `requests.compat` at line 43 and used only as the `except` clause type at line 911. However, `RequestsJSONDecodeError` (imported at line 34) is itself a subclass of `CompatJSONDecodeError`, which means catching `JSONDecodeError` from compat is correct. The naming is confusing: two nearly identically named symbols (`JSONDecodeError` from compat and `RequestsJSONDecodeError` from exceptions) are both in scope, increasing the risk of future mixups. Consider catching `(ValueError, json.JSONDecodeError)` explicitly or just consolidate to one name.

findings.title: PEP-8: inline comment should have two spaces before `#`
findings.detail: `if is_py2: # e is a ValueError` — single space before the `#`. PEP 8 requires two spaces before an inline comment. Minor style issue.
